summary: Hannes Mühleisen announces official DuckDB support for Delta Lake and the Unity Catalog at the Data + AI Summit 2024. He positions DuckDB as filling the gap between Excel and Spark for last-mile data analysis. The Delta Lake integration leverages Databricks' delta kernel rather than building from scratch. He also announces DuckDB community extensions, allowing anyone to publish and share extensions easily.
key_points: DuckDB 1.0 released after six years of R&D, with stable SQL dialect, APIs, and backwards-compatible storage format
DuckDB fills the gap between Excel and Spark for datasets too large for spreadsheets but too small for distributed computing
Delta Lake integration uses the delta kernel library rather than a from-scratch implementation, delegating file-reading complexity
Community extensions launched, enabling anyone to build and publish DuckDB extensions for easy installation
money_quotes: DuckDB is really perfect for this last mile of data analysis, where you may not need a whole data center to compute something.
It was the culmination of six years of R&D in data management systems.
How are we going to do that? Obviously, we're going to use the best tool for the job available, right? CSV files. Maybe not.
One thing that's really special about the Delta Lake integration is that we use this delta kernel that Databricks is building with the community.

As an open source project and a small team, we're really excited about Tabular and Databricks bringing Delta Lake and Iceberg closer together because for us, it means we don't have to maintain two different things for essentially the same problem.
It means less work for us and I think everyone wins.
key_insight: DuckDB's Delta Lake integration via the shared delta kernel exemplifies how open ecosystem collaboration reduces duplicated effort and benefits everyone, especially small teams building in-process analytics tools.
